patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/2] kernel/entry: add CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH to compile SUD out

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/2] kernel/entry: add CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH to compile SUD out

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/2] kernel/entry: add kernel.syscall_user_dispatch sysctl
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysctl (kernel.syscall_user_dispatch) to control whether userspace can arm Syscall User Dispatch (SUD). The default value is 1, allowing arming of SUD. Disarming SUD is always permitted. The sysctl is only present when the kernel is built with CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH. This patch also updates documentation and code to reflect the new sysctl.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new configuration option, CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, to make Syscall User Dispatch (SUD) an optional feature in the Linux kernel. SUD allows system calls to be redirected to userspace handlers and is required by native foreign-binary emulators like Wine/Proton. The patch introduces this new configuration option to allow minimal or high-security systems to disable SUD if needed, while still making it available for systems that require it.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/vmstat: fold stranded per-cpu node stats when a node comes online

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/vmstat: fold stranded per-cpu node stats when a node comes online

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a long-standing issue where per-cpu node statistics are not properly folded when a node comes online, leading to permanent skew in the vmstat counters. The existing code zeroes out the per-cpu counters, causing a permanent loss of data. This patch folds the stranded deltas before the node rejoins the online set, ensuring accurate and consistent statistics.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the safety of folding stranded per-cpu node statistics during offlining, suggesting that it could lead to deadlocks or other issues. Also questioned the need for visiting every possible CPU.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, 27 Jun 2026 16:22:43 -0400 Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net> wrote:

> A per-node vmstat counter is pgdat->vm_stat[] plus per-cpu deltas.
> A balanced counter can sit split as global=+N / per-cpu=-N.
> 
> The folds reconciling the split only walk online nodes, so when
> try_offline_node() marks a node offline the per-cpu deltas are stranded.
> 
> A subsequent online resets the per-cpu area but not pgdat->vm_stat[],
> orphaning the +N permanently.  All NR_VM_NODE_STAT_ITEMS are affected.

Geeze, simple mistake, been there ten years...

> The existing code zeroes the per-cpu counters and causes a permanent
> skew. Fold the stranded deltas instead, before the node rejoins the
> online set. The node is not online yet and the hotplug lock is held,
> so the remote access to per-cpu values is safe.

Oh.  Shouldn't we be doing this during offlining?

> Discovered when node compaction hung for a nearly empty node, as the
> math to determine throttling broke.  Reproduced by repeated memory
> hotplug/unplug cycles on a node under pressure: NR_ISOLATED_ANON
> ratchets up and never returns to zero.
> 
> ...
>
> --- a/mm/mm_init.c
> +++ b/mm/mm_init.c
> @@ -1536,7 +1536,7 @@ void __ref free_area_init_core_hotplug(struct pglist_data *pgdat)
>  {
>  	int nid = pgdat->node_id;
>  	enum zone_type z;
> -	int cpu;
> +	int cpu, i;
>  
>  	pgdat_init_internals(pgdat);
>  
> @@ -1554,10 +1554,17 @@ void __ref free_area_init_core_hotplug(struct pglist_data *pgdat)
>  	pgdat->node_start_pfn = 0;
>  	pgdat->node_present_pages = 0;
>  
> -	for_each_online_cpu(cpu) {
> -		struct per_cpu_nodestat *p;
> +	/*
> +	 * Hot-unplug can leave per-cpu vmstat deltas unfolded (folders skip
> +	 * offline nodes) - reconcile this at online. Foreign access to counters
> +	 * is safe: the node is not online yet and we hold the hotplug lock.
> +	 */
> +	for_each_possible_cpu(cpu) {

That's a lot of CPUs

> +		struct per_cpu_nodestat *p = per_cpu_ptr(pgdat->per_cpu_nodestats, cpu);
>  
> -		p = per_cpu_ptr(pgdat->per_cpu_nodestats, cpu);
> +		for (i = 0; i < NR_VM_NODE_STAT_ITEMS; i++)

and that's a lot of items.

I guess the overall loop count won't be large enough to cause issues,
but it's large!

Perhaps there's some simple test we can do on the per_cpu_nodestat to
avoid the inner loop?  Perhaps might need to add a field for this?

btw, "for(int i..." is allowed nowadays.  It'll make this code nicer, IMO.

And... Sashiko seems to have found a pre-existing issue:
	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260627202243.758289-1-gourry@gourry.net

> +			if (p->vm_node_stat_diff[i])
> +				node_page_state_add(p->vm_node_stat_diff[i], pgdat, i);
>  		memset(p, 0, sizeof(*p));
>  	}

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Responded to Andrew Morton's concerns, explaining the difficulties of safely folding stranded per-cpu node statistics during offlining and the potential risks of visiting every possible CPU.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, Jun 27, 2026 at 04:10:07PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> 
> > The existing code zeroes the per-cpu counters and causes a permanent
> > skew. Fold the stranded deltas instead, before the node rejoins the
> > online set. The node is not online yet and the hotplug lock is held,
> > so the remote access to per-cpu values is safe.
> 
> Oh.  Shouldn't we be doing this during offlining?
>

I tried this first.  I was unable to convince myself there was a 
safe way to accomplish this.

1) sashiko pointed out we can't schedule_on_each_cpu while holding
   the hotplug lock because we'll re-take cpus_read_lock and cause
   a deadlock condition with cpu-hotplug.

2) I'm not sure we can do it after the hotplug lock as been dropped,
   at least not safely.  At the very least another hot-plug re-adding
   the node could start.  That just seemed like a bad path.

3) foreign cpu access to the per-cpu values are not atomic with
   respect to in-flight folds on the target cpu.  this_cpu_xchg
   and this_cpu_add are (i believe) only atomic wrt the cpu itself
   (can't be interrupted mid-exchange).

doing it before node_offline() has problems (in-flight folds),
doing it after node_offline() still *technically* carries the same
in-flight fold risk - just narrower (fold has to have started already).

I couldn't convince myself there wasn't still a race, so here we are.

> > +	for_each_possible_cpu(cpu) {
> 
> That's a lot of CPUs
> 

Unfortunately - cpus may have gone offline while the node was offline,
so we legitimately have to visit every *possible* cpu :[

> > +		struct per_cpu_nodestat *p = per_cpu_ptr(pgdat->per_cpu_nodestats, cpu);
> >  
> > -		p = per_cpu_ptr(pgdat->per_cpu_nodestats, cpu);
> > +		for (i = 0; i < NR_VM_NODE_STAT_ITEMS; i++)
> 
> and that's a lot of items.

I am aware :[.  I suppose we could vectorize the collection here on some
archs, but I try to avoid being clever where I can.

> 
> I guess the overall loop count won't be large enough to cause issues,
> but it's large!
> 
> Perhaps there's some simple test we can do on the per_cpu_nodestat to
> avoid the inner loop?  Perhaps might need to add a field for this?

Hadn't considered this, but maybe.  Will take a look.

> 
> btw, "for(int i..." is allowed nowadays.  It'll make this code nicer, IMO.
> 

aye aye o7

> And... Sashiko seems to have found a pre-existing issue:
> 	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260627202243.758289-1-gourry@gourry.net
> 

Will take a look, thanks!

~Gregory

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/vmstat: flush per-cpu node stats when a node goes offline

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/vmstat: flush per-cpu node stats when a node goes offline

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a problem where per-cpu node stats become stranded when a node goes offline, causing inconsistent vmstat values. The solution involves flushing the per-cpu deltas before the node leaves the online set, ensuring that the global counters accurately reflect the system's state.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential deadlock issue with concurrent cpu hotplug and the sync code. Realized that zeroing per-cpu stats at online time is the root cause of the problem, not the solution.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, Jun 27, 2026 at 03:31:07AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
>  
>  	/*
>  	 * all memory/cpu of this node are removed, we can offline this
> -	 * node now.
> +	 * node now.  Fold any pending per-cpu vmstat diffs into the global
> +	 * counters first: once the node leaves the online set the periodic
> +	 * fold skips it, orphaning the residual on a later online.
>  	 */
> +	sync_vm_stats();

Sashiko points out this can deadlock on concurrent cpu hotplug, which
after a quick look seems accurate.

Has also made me realize the sync code is also racy with cpu hotplug
as well as it iterates per-online-cpu.

There are probably more races like this out there.

Will need to give this a little more thought.

>  	node_set_offline(nid);
>  	unregister_node(nid);
>  }

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/vmstat: flush per-cpu node stats when a node goes offline
Re: [PATCH] mm/vmstat: flush per-cpu node stats when a node goes offline

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] mm: __access_remote_vm with per-VMA lock

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/3] x86/mm: use READ_ONCE/WRITE_ONCE for mm->context.untag_mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the x86 Linux kernel to use READ_ONCE and WRITE_ONCE macros for accessing and modifying mm->context.untag_mask, a single aligned word that is written once at LAM enablement time and remains stable thereafter. The changes are made in three files: arch/x86/include/asm/mmu_context.h, arch/x86/include/asm/uaccess_64.h, and arch/x86/kernel/process_64.c. The patch annotates the reads and writes to make lockless access explicit and prevent compiler reloads or tearing of the field. No functional changes are introduced by this patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/3] mm/pagewalk: let folio_walk_start() run under the per-VMA lock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows callers of folio_walk_start() to run under the per-VMA lock instead of the mmap lock, reducing contention and improving performance for readers that only need to access a single page. The patch adds a new flag FW_VMA_LOCKED, which requires CONFIG_MMU_GATHER_RCU_TABLE_FREE and excludes hugetlb VMAs. Callers must keep interrupts disabled until folio_walk_end(). This change does not affect existing callers, as none currently pass the FW_VMA_LOCKED flag.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes expressed frustration that the patch author did not respond to their review, and requested a response.

The reviewer pointed out that the patch's description of munmap() behavior is incorrect, as it acquires the VMA lock and only removes upper-level tables if the entire range is spanned. They also stated that PTE page tables can be removed via MADV_DONTNEED under RCU, making the GUP fast imitating mechanism unnecessary.

Reviewer noted that the patch's assumption about CONFIG_MMU_GATHER_RCU_TABLE_FREE is incorrect, as retract_page_tables() and try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp() use pte_free_defer(), which uses RCU instead of serializing against lockless walkers.

Reviewer questioned the validity of adding FW_VMA_LOCKED flag, suggesting that they may be misunderstanding the patch's purpose or that there might be an issue with the locking mechanism.

The reviewer found the patch description to be overly verbose and requested that it be edited for readability
review_comments.raw_body:
Rik, it really would have helped if you'd replied to review :)

On Wed, Jun 24, 2026 at 09:50:52PM -0400, Rik van Riel wrote:

---

Yeah I need to update the documentation on this at
https://docs.kernel.org/mm/process_addrs.html it's more subtle than written
there.

Firstly you're wrong about munmap() - it acquires the VMA lock of the VMAs freed
in the range and will only remove an upper level table if the entire range is
spanned.

And that's the only way higher level tables can be removed.

PTE page tables can be removed via MADV_DONTNEED, but that a. acquires the VMA
lock and b. frees the PTE page table under RCU.

A THP collapse can happen concurrently, but PTEs are freed under RCU so you
don't need to do this GUP fast imitating stuff.

---

retract_page_tables() -> pte_free_defer() -> RCU
try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp() -> pte_free_defer() -> RCU

---

Yes it is?

I mean unless I'm missing something here.

---

This is an unreadable wall of text, if it's AI generated please edit before
sending.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledges a potential issue with khugepaged's handling of page tables during THP collapse, noting that relying solely on the RCU read lock could result in seeing old page table contents that no longer point to current process memory. The author suggests two possible solutions for khugepaged and asks if they are missing something.
review_comments.raw_body:
One issue here is that while we can safely read
the old page table under the RCU read lock, in
the middle of a THP collapse there is no guarantee
that the old page table points at the process's 
current memory.

Khugepaged could fix this in one of two ways:
- zap all readers with an IPI, and use that as
  synchronization
- make sure the old page table's PTEs point at
  the individual pages inside the new PMD

Right now khugepaged does the first.

Relying only on the RCU read lock to read the
page table could result in us seeing old page
table contents, that no longer point at the
current process memory.

Unless I'm missing something...

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/3] mm: read remote memory without the mmap lock where possible
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds an opportunistic, read-only fast path to access remote memory in Linux. The fast path takes the per-VMA lock and copies resident pages out using a page table walk run with interrupts disabled. If the request spans multiple VMAs or if permission checks fail, it falls back to the mmap_lock path for validation. This patch aims to reduce contention on large machines by avoiding unnecessary locking of the mmap lock in common cases where memory is already resident.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes expressed disagreement with the patch, stating that he doesn't think the changes are necessary and requested further discussion in the review.

Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes requested that patch descriptions be kept concise and readable by using paragraphs instead of a wall of text.
review_comments.raw_body:
As I said in reply to 2/3 I don't think you need to do this.

Let's have a discussion _in the review_ about it :)

---

No wall of text please :)

Same comments for the rest, please use paragraphs, try to be succinct, etc.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a fast path for __access_remote_vm that uses per-VMA locks instead of the process-wide mmap_lock, improving system monitoring reliability and speeding up the function under contention. The fast path is only enabled when it's safe to do so, requiring a kernel with per-VMA locks, RCU page table freeing, and specific VMA types excluded. This change aims to address issues where processes get stuck holding the mmap_lock for extended periods, hindering system monitoring tools' ability to assess and log situations.

review_comments.summary: Reviewer David expressed strong disagreement with the patch and requested a NACK due to concerns that the patch does not address his previous comments about not reimplementing GUP entirely.
review_comments.raw_body:
You mean, ignoring my comments about not reiplementing GUP entirely?

NAK

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch series should be sent after the merge window closes and not during it

Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes expressed dissatisfaction with the patch description, stating that it does not clearly attribute changes to specific reviewers and implying that the author has not adequately addressed previous comments.

Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes expressed agreement with the patch but emphasized the importance of responding to reviews inline and having discussions to ensure progress is made correctly, as he has limited review time and prefers triage by discussion rather than dismissal.
review_comments.raw_body:
(Note that it's the merge window, really ideally you should be saving sending
non-RFC series until after the merge window closes, none of these series will be
taken yet and you'll have to resend anyway)

This is really not sufficient :) you should break out the changes you
make and who suggested them.

If people give their time to review, you should take the time to make it clear
you've dealt with it.

---

Yeah agreed.

As replied elsewhere, I don't think we need to do that anyway?

But you _really_ need to respond to review inline so we can have those
discussions and ensure that you're moving forward in the correct way.

Just quietly resending with a catch-all comment as per above puts all the load

On my side, my review time is very limited now, so triage dictates general
dismissals when the series looks wrong, engaging in discussion will help us all
move forward efficiently.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the reliability of system monitoring under mmap_lock contention, specifically during THP collapse. They acknowledge that relying only on the RCU read lock to read the page table could result in seeing old page table contents that no longer point at the current process memory. The author suggests two possible ways for khugepaged to fix this issue and notes that it currently uses the first approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
One issue here is that while we can safely read
the old page table under the RCU read lock, in
the middle of a THP collapse there is no guarantee
that the old page table points at the process's 
current memory.

Khugepaged could fix this in one of two ways:
- zap all readers with an IPI, and use that as
  synchronization
- make sure the old page table's PTEs point at
  the individual pages inside the new PMD

Right now khugepaged does the first.

Relying only on the RCU read lock to read the
page table could result in us seeing old page
table contents, that no longer point at the
current process memory.

Unless I'm missing something...

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about the feasibility of implementing a mmap_lock-less GUP, expressed willingness to work on it but wants to understand prerequisites and acceptable implementation before proceeding.
review_comments.raw_body:
Do we actually have a path to doing that?

I misread that as more of a wish list thing, not
as something we could realistically do today.

How would I go about making that mmap_lock-less
GUP a reality?

What are the prerequisites?

I'm not opposed to working on that, but I
would like to figure out ahead of time
what an acceptable implementation would
roughly look like.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David (Arm) expressed concerns about the patch's potential to introduce unnecessary complexity, specifically in GUP users, and requested that alternatives be explored before proceeding.

Reviewer David (Arm) expressed confusion and skepticism about the patch's intention to add a fast path for __access_remote_vm under mmap_lock contention, questioning how the current implementation aligns with the stated goal of not doing something 'unless inevitable'.

Reviewer David expressed frustration and disappointment with the patch's reimplementation of a GUP-like interface, suggesting that it is not necessary and may be overly complex. He requests someone with GUP experience to properly rethink the lookup+fault path instead of adding workarounds.
review_comments.raw_body:
Well, I know what we do not want to reimplement GUP in some GUP users unless
really unavoidable. And I want that explored first before moving further into
this growing complexity in a GUP users.

---

I really don't know how you concluded that.

I mean, I know, my English is bad, but it's hard for me to get how

	"Yeah, we're not doing that unless inevitable."

is a wish list.

Yeah, well, ok, my kids treat also a "no" as a wish list ;)

---

Did you actually read at least some of the stuff I wrote in my mails? You know,
the ones where you didn't reply to? :)

Seeing a v2 with some weird gup-fast-like looking reimplementatiom (eww) is not
something I enjoyed at this point. We don't need another set of special page
table walkers.

We really need someone to look into this with some GUP experience or the
willingness to properly think the GUP lookup+fault path through, instead of
adding some creative workarounds to selective GUP user.

I will try to find some time to think it through, but my time would be better
spent guiding someone (and definitely not someones LLM) to understand

(1) which interface we could start with (as I said, a GUP interface where we
pass a VMA-lcoked / mm-read-lcoked VMA instead of the MM)

(2) which faults we could automatically resolve under VMA lock (I mentioned
userfaultfd is tricky but the existing GUP call already doesn't trigger uffd)

(3) whether gup-fast could be reused to some degree, or what it would take in
order to do that.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author identified __access_remote_vm as a suitable starting point for implementing per-VMA locks, citing its pain points and high caller count.

Author addressed David's concern that normal pages could be accessed using only per-VMA locks, proposing to implement a locking function for __access_remote_vm() that takes the appropriate lock and handles expand_stack() calls. The author suggests taking the slow path for accesses spanning multiple VMAs and adjusting locking assertions in related functions.

Author is addressing David's (Arm) feedback on whether __access_remote_vm can use gup-fast for accesses entirely within a single VMA, and considering switching to gup-fast as an alternative to eliding the first VMA lookup in get_user_pages. Author acknowledges that this makes things clearer but asks if there are any major issues overlooked.
sentiment_signals: focusing on one path at a time
review_comments.raw_body:
__access_remote_vm seems like a decent place to start,
since that is a pain point for several people, and also
the path into remote get_user_pages with the most callers.

After that there are a few more callers in performance
sensitive paths:
- execve / argv setup -> new mm, no lock contention?
- KVM async pagefault path -> most/all of these to the same VMA?
- make_device_exclusive -> ???
- pin_user_pages_remote -> probably a good target?

---

It looks like for normal pages, any access that
does not require expanding the stack could be
done using just the per-VMA locks.

That might allow us to implement a locking
function for __access_remote_vm() that
takes the appropriate lock for the situation,
and also takes care of the expand_stack() 
calls, if needed.

We could also take the slow path for an
access that spans multiple VMAs.

Then we could have an inner loop that does
only the page accessing and copying, with
no calls to expand_stack() in the while (len)
loop.

At the end of __access_remote_vm() we can
then unlock according to the way we locked.

Adjusting the locking assertions in __get_user_pages_locked
and untagged_addr_remote to allow the per-VMA lock seem
fairly straightforward.

Passing the starting vma all the way into __get_user_pages
would also allow us to skip looking up the VMA a second
time after the first lookup in __access_remote_vm().

---

If an access is entirely inside a single VMA,
it looks like using gup-fast could be possible?

The whole "look up VMA permissions" thing inside
get_user_pages() should not be necessary for callers
like __access_remote_vm() that have already done
that.

That could be a nice speedup for thing like
ptrace, BPF copy_from_user_task, etc

Another question is whether switching right
to gup-fast would be cleaner than trying to
elide the first VMA lookup from __get_user_pages,
but answering that may require looking at some
prototype code and figuring out more of the
details.

Thank you for asking the questions above.
Looking into those made things a little
clearer.

This looks a whole lot more manageable now.

Are there any major things I overlooked?

Are there any pending patches in -mm or
elsewhere that I should pull into my tree
before starting?

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary: Reviewer emphasized the importance of engaging with reviewers during the review process to ensure that all comments and concerns are addressed before proceeding with further changes.
sentiment_signals: reviewer emphasized the importance of engagement
reviewer highlighted potential consequences of not addressing comments
review_comments.raw_body:
I agree with everything David said here.

In general Rik do make sure to engage with reviewers during the review and reply
to points raised so we can have a healthy discussion (I see you replied to me
thanks for that! So hopefully this is now resolved).

It'll save you going off and doing X, Y + Z in contradiction of comments you
perhaps missed or felt were nice-to-haves, only to find that they
were in fact critical blockers that prevent your series from being merged.

With review workload as it is this not only saves you time, stress + energy, but
saves your reviewers from the same :)

Generally if there's feedback, response to all of it is expected and really
required, albeit obviously for reasons of economy an aggregrate response or
implied acks on nits are fine.

Thanks, Lorenzo

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary: Reviewer asked the submitter to include a diffstat in the cover letter and adjust their tooling to do so, noting that git format-patch --cover-letter already does this by default.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please always send a diffstat in your cover letter by the way.

I noticed you didn't do this elsewhere also, so please adjust your tooling to do
this :) (git format-patch --cover-letter does this by default).

Thanks, Lorenzo
